paragraphs.text: Prediction: L-Aspartic acid is the most likely amino acid (among those not covered in our Zn²⁺ catalog snapshot) to form the strongest zinc complex. It offers two carboxylate O donors that are both deprotonated near neutral pH plus the backbone amine, enabling chelation and high effective denticity. This combination generally yields larger formation constants with Zn²⁺ than amino acids whose side chains are neutral (serine/threonine, methionine) or protonated (lysine/arginine) under the same conditions.

under the same conditions.

paragraphs.text:
Context from SRD‑46:
- Our Zn²⁺ system catalog snapshot includes strong donors already studied: histidine (imidazole N; ligand_5898) and cysteine (thiolate S; ligand_5856), each showing multiple MLₙ species and hydroxo equilibria, consistent with high stability for soft/borderline donors.
- Glycine and alanine (simple amino acids) are also covered and form ML, ML₂, ML₃ species with Zn²⁺, illustrating the common N,O chelation motif.
- Among remaining canonical amino acids, aspartate/glutamate uniquely have a second carboxylate with low pKa (3.71 for Asp; 4.15 for Glu), ensuring anionic donors at neutral pH and enabling bidentate/tridentate coordination with the backbone amine. In contrast, tyrosine’s phenolic donor requires pH ≥10; serine/threonine are weak neutral donors; methionine’s thioether is softer but neutral and generally weaker than cysteine’s thiolate; lysine/arginine side chains are cationic and poor donors at neutral pH.

paragraphs.text:
Chemistry insight:
- Zn²⁺ is a borderline hard/soft Lewis acid that displays strong affinity for anionic donors and chelates. With histidine (N) and cysteine (S) already covered, the next best readily accessible donor set among natural amino acids is the di‑carboxylate of aspartate, which can chelate via O,O (and optionally N) at physiological pH. Glutamate should be similar but slightly less favorable at lower pH because its side‑chain pKa is higher.

Zn²⁺ is a borderline hard/soft Lewis acid
displays strong affinity for anionic donors
and chelates.
With histidine (N) and cysteine (S) already covered,
the next best readily accessible donor set among natural amino acids is the di‑carboxylate of aspartate,
which can chelate via O,O (and optionally N) at physiological pH.
Glutamate should be similar
but slightly less favorable at lower pH
its side‑chain pKa is higher.

paragraphs.text:
Conclusion:
- Most probable top new binder: L‑Aspartic acid (ligand_5802) ≥ L‑Glutamic acid (ligand_5804).
- Rationale: multiple anionic O donors available near neutral pH and strong chelation geometry; competing candidates (methionine, tyrosine, serine/threonine, lysine/arginine) either remain neutral, require high pH to deprotonate, or are protonated and thus weaker donors for Zn²⁺.

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: “Strong chelation geometry” is general coordination chemistry knowledge; SRD-46 tables do not encode geometry.
Example: ligand_5863 | … (Methionine) — other listed amino acids also appear in the tool outputs (see snippets).

paragraphs.text: Source: NIST SRD‑46 (data shown above: ligand IDs, donor types, and pKa brackets). To fully confirm “not yet studied” status across the entire database, a comprehensive Zn²⁺ stability search should be run for all amino acids; our prediction uses the catalog snapshot and pKa trends.
